name: Typer
what_it_does: Python CLI framework built on Click. Uses Python type annotations to define CLI arguments and options — no decorators needed for simple cases. Auto-generates help text from type hints and docstrings. Supports auto-completion, Rich integration for beautiful output, and subcommands. Created by FastAPI's author as the CLI equivalent of FastAPI.
use_cases: Build Python agent CLI tools with type-safe argument parsing using standard Python annotations
Create multi-subcommand agent CLIs (agent run, agent configure, agent deploy) with minimal boilerplate
Auto-generate shell completions and documentation for agent CLI tools from type annotations
Integrate Rich terminal output in Python agent CLIs using Typer's built-in Rich support
Build agent scripts that work both as library functions and CLI commands with the same Python code
not_for: Complex CLI customization requiring Click internals — Typer abstracts Click but exposes escape hatches; heavy Click customization may be easier with Click directly
Non-Python CLI tools — Typer is Python-only
Performance-critical startup — Typer's import overhead (Click + Rich) may be notable for sub-10ms startup scripts
best_when: You're building a Python CLI agent and want type-safe argument parsing with minimal boilerplate, using Python type annotations you already know from FastAPI/Pydantic.
avoid_when: You need very simple one-command CLIs — argparse from stdlib or Click directly may be sufficient; or you need advanced Click features that Typer's abstraction doesn't expose cleanly.

pricing.notes: MIT license. Maintained by Sebastián Ramírez (FastAPI author).

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — startup-time argument parsing only.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Type mismatch errors include expected type and received value. Missing required argument errors name the argument. Help text is auto-generated from type hints and docstrings.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: CLI argument parsing — deterministic, no side effects from framework.
known_agent_gotchas: Typer uses Python type annotations for argument types — Optional[str] creates an optional argument, while str creates a required one; be precise about Optional vs. required
Default values in function signatures become CLI defaults — None default makes an argument optional; a non-None default makes it an option with a default value
Typer subcommands use separate functions decorated with @app.command() — calling one subcommand from another requires using typer.run() or restructuring as library functions
Boolean flags use --flag/--no-flag convention by default — bare bool type creates a required bool argument, not a flag; use bool = typer.Option(False) for flag-style options
typer.Argument vs typer.Option: Arguments are positional (no --name prefix), Options use -- prefix — the distinction is important for user-facing CLI design
Typer 0.9+ changed the callback behavior for app-level callbacks — global options (--verbose, --config) using callbacks must use is_eager=True to run before subcommand parsing

security_score_components.security_notes: CLI framework. Sensitive values passed as CLI args appear in process lists and shell history — prefer environment variables for secrets. Use typer.Option(envvar='SECRET') for env var sourcing.
